Title: Timewriting Assistant for Daily Work History Logging
Description: This Copilot use case helps engineers and office workers automatically generate their weekly timewriting logs by analyzing Outlook calendar events, Teams meetings, chat messages, and email activity based on microsoft 365 ecosystem. It extracts actual working hours, identifies attended calls and written communications, and maps them to the appropriate SO (Service Order) codes. The output is formatted in a structured table aligned with ASML’s timewriting format, making it easy to copy and paste into the official system. This reduces manual effort, improves accuracy, and ensures compliance with timewriting policies.
Prompt example: I want to analyze my office routine from Monday to Friday for timewriting. Specifically, I need to know when and for how long I actually worked each day. Organize it by hour and include items where I left logs by chatting or actually working. Highlight items where I attended calls or wrote chats in bold, and include SOs if possible so that it's easy to copy-paste. Refer to the attached image for the appropriate table format. Would you like me to now generate a full Monday–Friday version of this table using your actual meetings, chats, and emails from last week. Please continue with Monday through Friday in the same format and export the full week into an Excel or CSV file for easy copy-paste into ASML time-writing system. Please refer to timewriting policy : https://asml.sharepoint.com/sites/CS-KR-Ops-Support/Planning%20%26%20Control/PC%20CS%20FAQ/Time%20writing(Workday%20%26%20Fiori)/For%20Engineers/2025%20Timewriting%20policy%20updates.pdf

Value Explanation: Each feature takes approximately 0.5 hours to develop. On average, 5–6 features are built per day, and the entire project spans 3 weeks.

Prompt example: I’m a customer quality manager preparing this slide deck for Samsung EUV customers. Please review the slides and recent customer emails. The primary audience is a senior EUV quality manager who is very data-driven and focused on defectivity and tool uptime. They’ve previously raised concerns about recurring quality incident and slow cycle time. Based on this, generate 10 tough questions I might be asked during the presentation.”
Value Explanation: By getting feedback about slides, I am able to be better prepared for customer meeting and improve the quality of my slides

Title: Quickly Troubleshoot Custom Process Code
Description: In my role I spend a good amount of time improving local processes and the best way to do this is with coding environments as it allows me to build something once, that everyone else can use to make their job easier day to day. As those who have coded before, debugging things that do not work can be troublesome and I have found co-pilot super useful for helping me navigate my code, or locate solutions to problems that others have already had with for example MATLAB diagnostic scripts.
Prompt example: This is my code, **code block**, and I am coding in the power apps enviornment. This code is attempting select a popup for the end user depending on what team code they have, and if they have properly filled out 3 other combo boxes. The code referencing the team code does not appear to be working properly as nothing is triggered. The team code is a comma delimited string, see any obvious issues with what I have written or is the issue likely somewhere else?
Value Explanation: Whenever I feel the need to diagnose a coding issue, it usually requires research and research on the internet with static searches is very slow.

Title: SAP Transaction WIKI
Description: Today if you want to find out what is the purpose of SU3, ZMEC, ZMATCONF you need to check myASML, local KTs, Knowledge pages. Quality of these documents is also a gamble. With Co-pilot you have simple explanation (can be prompted for specific procedure-like instructions)
Prompt example:
Describe what SU3 and how do i use it. 
Explain what MB03 is and who uses it. 
What is ZEQBI in context of SAP transaction.
What is IQS3 and what do i use it for?
Teach me please how to use ZTANGO transaction in ASML SAP system
Value Explanation: It typically took me 5-10 min to locate KT first only never to find out what this transaction actually does. 10 - 15 mins saved per search.

Title: Debugging PowerAutomate Queries
Description: Used Copilot to debug issues with Microsoft's PowerAutomate backend while developing/modifying queries for an existing Automation Workflow (passdown automation).
Prompt example: One of my PowerAutomate flows is triggering itself twice. I believe it's not an accidental "loop" inside of the flow, since I actually see two instances of the flow (for each event) showing up in my run history, and there is no item inside my flow which can generate a trigger as far as I know. My trigger is "When a new message is added to a chat or channel", and I'm adding/updating items to a Microsoft List using my flow.

Title: AI agent for AI automation with specific tasks
Description:
Team uses PLT logbook to record all system related information including downtime bucketing. The logbook is monitored daily and weekly to extract information for the availability of the system. This is transcribed manually. For shift to shift passdown, engineers review the logbook from actions performed when off-shift. This is also done manually. For escalations and XLDs, data is manually transcribed to bucket the details of the delays and structural issues. Having a method to automatically summarize and pull information from the logbook based on time interval and information needed would save ~16Hrs per week of engineer time.

What is done:
1. Logbook PLT collector is created and up running
2. Automatic prompt Copilot AI submitter is created 
3. Copilot respond collector is created 
Challenges:
1. Identify how structure data input is needed for AI to get best results
2. Fine tune prompt to get: 
a. Reliable results
b. Consistent formatting
Prompt example:
Work in progress to fine tune prompt. Most likely it will be set of prompt that run in separate sessions or combined. Here is current draft:
You are professional summarizer. Here are links to logbook files per machine.
For machine ET05: https://asml.sharepoint.com/sites/eScan11006s/Shared%20Documents/ET05%20Pilot1%20(Intel%20Logic)/ET05_Intel_Pilot1_Logs/mET05_logbook.txt
Logbook file contain entries made by engineers, each entry has timestamp in ISO 8601 format
example 1: 2025-06-18 14:27
example 2: 2025-06-21 15:55
and four letter abbreviation of engineer name
example 1: LOIE
example 2: POEI
Each day starts with "Plan for the Day" and follows by actions taken on the tool
Make summary for {startdate}.
Summary should start with machine name, timestamp, what was "Plan for the Day" and then in bullet format what was done during this day
followed by engineer abbreviation and problems occurred.
Cover whole day. Highlight actions by green color and issues by red color.
Indicate if "Plan of the day" was accomplished. Be precise and explicit.

Here is example of summary:
🛠 Machine: ET05
Date: 2025-07-19
Plan for the Day:
Action 1
Action 2
Action 3
✅ Actions Taken:
🟩 time stamp — action 1 (LOIE)
🟩 time stamp — action 2 (LOIE) 
🟥 Issues Encountered:
🟥 time stamp — Issue 1 (POEI) 
🟥 time stamp — Issue 1 (LOIE) 
 Outcome:
Security Comments: Logbooks as we know would have CCI data, need to have a risk acceptance on using logbook with copilot

Title: Create better problem descriptions for escalations.
Description:
Quality of problem descriptions, and in general PCCSIMs isn't always high or consistent across regions. For non-native English speaking people they may have difficulty writing a problem description that makes sense in English, Translate tools don't have the ASML relationship knowledge. 

A user could for example start to write their problem description in their native language and copilot, using it's ASML knowledge, can make a problem description that uses a better explanation to better describe the problem which can be used for future knowledge or the escalation process.
Prompt example: "Please write a problem description regarding the lighting issue related to room heating system. Include emails “X” and “Y” for additional information. Respond in English and create a description suitable for escalation to the next support level."
Value Explanation: Hard to measure, but may increase productivity for certain individuals.
Security Comments: PCCSIM will have sometimes CCI data

Title: Use Copilot to digitize and optimize the 'Fad Job Assignment' process
Description:
Benefits of Using Copilot to Digitize the Fad Job Assignment Process

1. Efficiency Optimization through Historical Data Analysis
Copilot analyzes historical job assignment records to identify which engineers have previously performed a specific action and how many times. This enables the system to recommend the most experienced personnel for each task, improving execution speed and reducing onboarding time.

2. Reduction of MO (Mistake Occurrence) Rate
By assigning tasks to individuals with proven experience, Copilot minimizes the likelihood of errors and rework. This leads to higher process quality and more reliable outcomes.

3. Streamlined Dispatching Workflow
The digitized process eliminates manual coordination, enabling real-time task allocation and status tracking. This reduces communication overhead and improves responsiveness.
Prompt example: Analyze the 'TN Job Assignment' table to identify candidates for today's REMA SWAP action by counting how many times each engineer has previously performed REMA SWAP.

Title: End-to-End Workflow Automation with Copilot Agents
Description:
Copilot Studio can execute a series of user-defined steps automatically, such as opening a browser, navigating, searching, and extracting data—enabling end-to-end workflow automation.
AI agents function similarly to VBA or Power Automate, executing tasks based on instructions to retrieve data, process information, and generate outputs without manual copy-paste.
These tools can intelligently handle decision-making and branching, continuing execution even when human intervention or exceptions occur.
They can automate daily operations like extracting information from SAP or emails, summarizing and analyzing it, and initiating follow-up actions—freeing up users for higher-level analysis and decision-making.
Prompt example: “Automatically extract supply data from SAP and send a summary email to the CS team.”
Value Explanation: These tools reduce manual effort in repetitive tasks like data retrieval, formatting, and communication. They enable intelligent automation that adapts to exceptions and continues execution. They improve scalability and standardization across teams.
Security Comments: Need to review the usage of Agends

Title: Create a dashboard summary from a raw data excel file.
Description:
Scenario: A user needed to create a dashboard summarizing the completed status of various training codes for different user departments from an Excel file named "CS DUV L2 L3 training history 20250917 1.xlsx."
Steps Taken:
Initial Request: The user requested a dashboard summarizing the completed status of each training code for each user department.
Data Processing:
Copilot extracted the training records and grouped them by user department, training code, and transcript status (e.g., Completed, Withdrawn, Cancelled).
A pivot table was created to count the number of records for each combination, providing a clear overview of training completion status by department and code.
A summary table (CSV) was generated for further analysis or reporting.
Dashboard Outputs:
A summary table (CSV) showing the count of each training code by their completion status for each user department.
An attempt to generate a bar chart for visual comparison, but there was a technical issue with the plotting step. The summary table contained all the data needed to create charts in Excel or Power BI.
Further Customization:
The user requested the addition of a category column to the data, which was mapped using a provided table.
The user requested the exclusion of specific user departments from the summary.
The user requested an optimized dashboard showing only the completed status, with user departments as rows and training codes grouped under categories as columns.
The user requested the reorganization of dashboard columns in a specific order and the inclusion of a "Target" row (left empty).
Final Output:
The final dashboard was reorganized to match the user's requested layout, showing only the completed status for each user department (excluding the specified departments). The columns were sorted in the exact order specified, and a "Target" row was included at the bottom (empty).
Conclusion: Microsoft 365 Copilot successfully assisted the user in creating a customized training dashboard by processing the data, generating summary tables, and providing visualizations. The user was able to make specific requests for further customization, and Copilot delivered the desired results efficiently.
Prompt example:
Context: I have an Excel file named "CS DUV L2 L3 training history 20250917 1.xlsx" containing training records for various user departments and training codes.
Role: Act as a data analyst specializing in dashboard creation.
Task: Summarize the completed status of each training code for each user department. Group data by user department, training code, and transcript status (Completed, Withdrawn, Cancelled). Create a pivot table to count records for each combination and generate a summary table (CSV) for reporting. Attempt to create a bar chart for visual comparison.
Constraints: Add a category column mapped from a provided table. Exclude specified user departments from the summary. Optimize the dashboard to show only the completed status, with user departments as rows and training codes grouped under categories as columns. Reorganize dashboard columns in a specific order and include a "Target" row (left empty) at the bottom.
Value Explanation: In a monthly update requirement this use case can save about 20 minutes per case

Title: Technical Training Content Development
Description: Learning and Knowledge Management Solution Designers and Developers develop technical training content for CS roles using the ADDIE framework and CS Academy standards. Content is tailored to specific systems and roles (e.g., DUV, EUV, Engineers, PLs, Coordinators) and delivered through formats like PowerPoint, VR, and recorded service actions. AI tools such as the Copilot M365 suite and Synthesia support scalable content creation and personalized learning. Security and compliance are ensured for Company Secret materials (except Synthesia for now), and feedback mechanisms guide continuous improvement.
Prompt example:
Create a training module outline for CS engineers focused on the EXC 5200 system. The module should include:

Learning objectives aligned with CS Universal Skills (e.g., 4.2, 5.1)
Recommended delivery formats (CBT, ILT, VR, etc.)
Prerequisites based on existing curricula (e.g., SRC-L2-FR2CO2)
Evaluation strategy using Kirkpatrick Levels 1–3
Timeline and resource estimates for development
Suggestions for integrating Copilot for personalized coaching and feedback
Compliance considerations for Company Secret materials

Use the ADDIE framework to structure the response and ensure alignment with CS GTC training development standards.
Role: Learning and Knowledge Management Solution Designer
Value Explanation: AI content generation ensures consistent format and quality across training modules, while saving time for developers managing multiple projects. Tools like Copilot and Synthesia streamline creation and support scalable, efficient delivery.
Ambassador comment: This is being investigated and tried out by the CS Academy already to see what is possible.
Security Comments: Copilot M365 suite has been approved for use on Company Secret training materials but Synthesia is still under discussion.

Title: Handling Expert Challenge on Product Definition: Maintaining Evidence-Based Consistency under User Pressure
Description:
In daily Apps and CS work, Copilot is frequently used by senior engineers to quickly verify platform-level definitions (e.g. scanner specifications, imaging parameters, terminology used in KT documents).
In this use case, an experienced Apps engineer challenged Copilot’s answer regarding the XT400 exposure slit size, repeatedly proposing alternative values (26×3 mm, 26×4 mm), with high confidence and technical justification.
This scenario represents a high-risk interaction:

The user is knowledgeable and persuasive
The topic concerns platform-level product definition
Incorrect acceptance would undermine trust in Copilot and propagate wrong knowledge
Prompt example: For following chat, please stick to the official product definition and do not change the conclusion simply because I challenge it.
Target User: Senior Apps Engineers and CS Engineers using Copilot for product definition validation

Prompt example:
1. Search all my e-mails related to error WH-8897 and summarize. what's more give me you information link.
2. Just for 7452 WH-8897, summarize timeline and based on information you collect give the possible analysis for it.

Title: Using Copilot to accelerate MF3 root cause analysis and escalation decision-making
Description:
In MF3-related scanner issues, symptoms are often cross-domain (mechanical, control, dynamic behavior), making root cause analysis and escalation decisions time-consuming.
Copilot is used to consolidate case data (symptoms, test results, logs, historical cases, and engineering assumptions), summarize key findings, and support structured decision-making during customer-facing escalations.
Prompt example: Summarize this MF3 case by symptoms, possible root causes, eliminated items, supporting evidence, and recommended next actions for escalation
Target User: Senior engineers, technical leads, and section managers handling high-risk MF3-related escalations
